topic: A musician hears their song being performed by someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence of the practice room was suddenly shattered by a sound that was undeniably mine. A melody, intricate and aching, poured out from the worn acoustic guitar of the stranger across the room. My breath hitched, a sharp, painful intake of air that seemed to steal the oxygen from the small space. I froze mid-note, my fingers stiff on the fretboard. It was *my* song. The lyrics, the specific, fragile chord progression—it was all laid bare, executed with a fluency I hadn't allowed myself to achieve in months. A hot flush crept up my neck, staining my cheeks crimson. I quickly looked down at my shoes, focusing intently on the scuff marks, desperate to become invisible. The performer finished the piece with a flourish, and the applause washed over the room, a wave of validation that felt like a physical blow. I mumbled an excuse about needing water, shoving my guitar case shut with unnecessary force, my heart hammering a frantic rhythm against my ribs.

[story 2]

I was nursing lukewarm coffee, trying to drown out the gnawing anxiety that had been building all week, when it happened. From the hallway, a voice—a voice that sounded like it belonged to someone else—began singing. It was my ballad, the one I’d poured my soul into, being sung with such effortless grace by a complete stranger. Every muscle in my body tensed. I felt a sudden, intense need to disappear, to melt into the wallpaper. My gaze darted toward the door, wanting to flee the scene entirely. When the song ended, the applause swelled, bright and loud. I felt a strange tightening in my chest, a heavy cloak settling over me. I gripped the mug so tightly my knuckles turned white, my jaw clenching so hard I could feel the strain in my molars. I managed a weak nod when someone passed, the effort of appearing normal draining every reserve of my composure.

[story 3]

The sound drifted through the thin walls, undeniable and heartbreakingly familiar. It was my composition, the one that felt too raw, too exposed, now being translated into breath and string by an unknown artist. I stood motionless by the window, watching the streetlights blur, trying to process the intrusion. A wave of profound awkwardness washed over me, making my skin feel clammy. I wanted the floor to swallow me whole. I could feel the heat rising from my core, a burning sensation that made me want to curl into a tight ball. When the rendition concluded, the energy in the space shifted, celebratory and appreciative. I turned away abruptly, walking toward the darkened corner of the room, my movements jerky and uncontrolled. I kept my eyes fixed on the floorboards, hoping the shadows would consume me before I had to face the reality of what I had just witnessed.
